steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which statement about hummingbird preferences is most strongly supported. The passage sets up an expectation (high-sugar flowers are thought to be especially attractive) and then overturns it: visit frequencies weren't influenced by nectar traits, and physical compatibility of corollas and bills more strongly predicted interactions.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Hummingbirds consistently prefer plant species whose flowers have physical traits that are compatible with their bills but offer relatively low nectar rewards.': "Consistently" overstates the finding, and the passage never says hummingbirds prefer low-nectar flowers — only that physical compatibility predicts visits better than nectar traits do. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The floral traits that most strongly influence which plant species hummingbirds prefer may result in certain hummingbird species visiting flowers with relatively low nectar rewards over those with higher nectar rewards.': If physical compatibility — not nectar content — drives visits, it follows that hummingbirds may end up at low-nectar flowers over high-nectar ones. "May result" correctly hedges the inference. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Hummingbirds’ preferences are primarily driven by trait-matching between their bills and flower corollas, though nectar qualities are a secondary factor driving their preferences.': The passage says visit frequencies weren't influenced by nectar traits at all — not that nectar is a secondary factor. This choice invents a residual role for nectar. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Physical compatibility between flower corollas and hummingbird bills becomes an important factor that influences hummingbird preferences when hummingbirds are uncertain about which plants produce high-reward nectar.': The passage mentions nothing about hummingbird uncertainty. This condition is invented. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
